1: Samuel’s farewell address — “I have listened to you in everything you asked of me and have set a king over you” — is the transition speech of the judge-prophet handing over to the king. Samuel as the departing leader who has served faithfully and now installs his successor is a type of John the Baptist: “he must become greater; I must become less” (John 3:30). The prophetic forerunner gives way to the king. John explicitly names himself as the “friend of the bridegroom” (John 3:29) — his joy is complete when the king arrives and the prophetic office yields to its fulfillment.
2: Samuel’s accounting of his life’s service — “I have served before you from my youth until this day” — is the righteousness claim of the faithful servant who can present himself before YHWH and the people with integrity. The servant who has served faithfully from youth and now faces transition: this is the type of Christ as YHWH’s servant who completes his commission and presents himself to the Father (John 17:4: “I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do”). Samuel’s old age stands in tension with Saul’s youth — the faithful old gives way to the new, as the old covenant gives way to the new in Christ.
3: A type: Samuel’s righteousness challenge — “testify against me... have I defrauded anyone? oppressed anyone? taken a bribe?” — is the challenge of perfect integrity. No accuser comes forward. Samuel’s clean hands are a type of the righteousness that Christ alone can claim perfectly: “can any of you prove me guilty of sin?” (John 8:46). Before Pilate, the verdict is similarly “I find no basis for a charge against him” (John 18:38). The righteous judge who invites accusation and receives none is the pattern of the one who is “holy, blameless, pure, set apart from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26). Samuel’s righteousness is genuine but limited; Christ’s is absolute and atoning.
4: The people’s testimony — “you have not defrauded us, oppressed us, or taken anything from anyone” — clears Samuel. The declaration of innocence by the people who were governed is the covenant community’s attestation. The NT parallel: the disciples’ testimony to Christ’s integrity (“we have seen his glory”, John 1:14), and the wider testimony that “he committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth” (1 Peter 2:22, citing Isaiah 53:9). The community’s attestation of the righteous leader’s integrity is the human side of the divine declaration of righteousness.
5: “The LORD and his anointed are witnesses today” — YHWH and the māšîaḥ together as witnesses. The pairing of YHWH and his anointed as co-witnesses is the OT formulation closest to the NT’s identification of Father and Son as one: “The Father and I are one” (John 10:30); “whoever has seen me has seen the Father” (John 14:9). The anointed one shares YHWH’s witness role — divine witness is not separated from the king’s witness. Christ as both divine and human witness to the truth (“I am the way, the truth...”, John 14:6; “for this reason I was born... to testify to the truth”, John 18:37) fulfills what the pairing of YHWH and his anointed anticipates.
6: Samuel invokes YHWH who “appointed Moses and Aaron” — the divine author of all legitimate prophetic and priestly appointment. The claim that YHWH himself appointed Moses and Aaron rather than Israel’s leaders choosing them establishes the principle of divine vocation versus human appointment. Christ’s high priesthood is similarly validated: “no one takes this honor on himself, but he receives it when called by God, just as Aaron was — and so Christ also did not take on himself the glory of becoming a high priest. But God said to him, ‘You are my Son’... you are a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek” (Hebrews 5:4-6). The divine appointment of Moses and Aaron is the precedent for the divine appointment of Christ.
7: Samuel calls Israel to “take your stand” while he rehearses YHWH’s righteous acts (ṣidqôt YHWH — the righteousness/justice-acts of YHWH). The recitation of divine ṣidqôt is the covenant lawsuit structure (the rîb): YHWH’s covenant acts are rehearsed to measure Israel’s response against them. Paul’s argument in Romans similarly rehearses God’s righteous acts (the covenant history of Abraham, Moses, and Christ) to establish the basis on which God declares sinners righteous. The ṣidqôt YHWH that Samuel recites in v7-11 are the type of the righteousness of God that is “revealed from faith to faith” in the gospel (Romans 1:17).
8: The Exodus pattern — Jacob into Egypt, the cry, Moses and Aaron sent, deliverance and settlement — is the foundational salvation narrative that the rest of the OT elaborates and the NT fulfills. Samuel’s rehearsal of this pattern frames the monarchy within the Exodus salvation: the king is YHWH’s instrument for continuing the Exodus work. Christ is the new Moses who leads the new Exodus from sin and death (Hebrews 3:1-6 compares Moses and Christ directly; John 6:32-33 contrasts Moses’ manna with the true bread from heaven). The salvation history that begins with the Exodus of Jacob’s family from Egypt reaches its terminus in Christ’s exodus from the grave.
9: Israel’s forgetting YHWH and being handed over to enemies — Sisera, the Philistines, Moab — is the Deuteronomic discipline cycle: forgetting → oppression → crying out → deliverance. This cycle runs throughout Judges and Samuel as the structure of covenant failure and grace. Paul describes the same structure in Romans 1:18-3:20: humanity forgot God, was given over to the consequences of that choice, and now cries out (1 Samuel 3:9-20: “no one is righteous”) — the universal version of the judges cycle. Christ enters this cycle not as another judge but as the one who absorbs the full weight of “being handed over” (Romans 4:25: “he was delivered over to death for our sins”) to break the cycle permanently.
10: The confession formula — “we have sinned; we forsook the LORD and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth” — is the covenant-repentance cry that activates divine rescue. The sequence of forsaking → confession → “deliver us now and we will serve you” is the shape of repentance and return. Luke 15’s prodigal son enacts precisely this: the son “comes to himself” (v17), confesses (“I have sinned against heaven and against you”, v18), and returns to the father. The father’s response — running to meet him before the confession is finished — is the grace that the judges cycle of Samuel only hints at. In Christ, the Father does not wait for the confession but goes to meet the returning sinner.
11: The four deliverers YHWH sent — Jerubbaal (Gideon), Bedan, Jephthah, Samuel — form a series of imperfect deliverers who each partially fulfil the pattern of the promised deliverer. The accumulation of judges, each saving Israel in their generation, creates the expectation of one who will save permanently. Heb 11’s “hall of faith” recounts these deliverers (Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, the prophets) to establish that “none of them received what had been promised, since God had planned something better for us so that only together with us would they be made perfect” (Hebrews 11:39-40). All the judge-deliverers point beyond themselves to Christ, the permanent and final deliverer.
12: “The LORD your God was your king” (waYHWH ʾelōhêkem malkəkem) — the theocratic claim that was rejected when Israel demanded a human king. The Psalms restore this claim: “The LORD is king” (Psalms 99:1); “The LORD, the Most High, is the great King over all the earth” (Psalms 47:2). Jesus comes preaching “the kingdom of God is at hand” (Mark 1:15) — the reinstatement of the rejected theocracy. John 19:14-15 frames the cross as the explicit rejection of YHWH’s kingship: “Here is your king!” — “we have no king but Caesar.” The statement that YHWH was Israel’s king is the constitution of the messianic kingdom that Christ comes to restore.
13: The conditional installation — “here is the king you chose and asked for; the LORD has set a king over you” — is the divine ratification of the people’s request with the warning conditions attached. The king is simultaneously the people’s choice and YHWH’s appointment — the human will and divine sovereignty working together, as in all covenant narrative. Christ similarly comes as both the one Israel longed for (“the consolation of Israel”, Luke 2:25) and the one the Father sends (“God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son”, John 3:16). The king given in response to a cry is given by divine will, not merely human wish.
14: The conditional covenant formula — “if you fear the LORD and serve him and obey his voice... both you and the king who rules you will follow the LORD your God” — states the ideal of the Davidic covenant: the king and the people together in covenantal faithfulness. The Psalms envision this ideal (Ps 72: the king who rules in justice and righteousness, under whom the people flourish). Christ fulfills the condition from both sides: as the true King he perfectly obeys the Father, and as the Head of the new covenant community he brings his people into that same obedience (“the obedience of faith”, Romans 1:5; 1 Samuel 16:26). The conditional covenant is fulfilled unconditionally in Christ because he meets the condition on behalf of all.
15: The covenant curse — “if you rebel against his command, the hand of the LORD will be against you, as it was against your ancestors” — is the Deuteronomic judgment that follows covenant breach. The hand of the LORD against the people is the same power that delivered them (Deuteronomy 26:8: “the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm”) when wielded in judgment rather than rescue. Christ absorbs the covenant curse: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written: Cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole” (Galatians 3:13, citing Deuteronomy 21:23). The hand that was against the covenant-breaker was laid on Christ, so that the hand of blessing might reach those who deserved the curse.
16: “Stand still and see the great thing the LORD is about to do before your eyes” — the invitation to witness divine action. Samuel uses the same formula as Moses at the Red Sea (Exodus 14:13: “stand firm and you will see the deliverance the LORD will bring you today”). The divine act about to be performed — unseasonable thunder and rain — is a sign of YHWH’s sovereign power over creation. “Stand still and see” is the posture of faith: not acting in self-reliance but waiting to witness what YHWH will do. The ultimate expression of “stand still and see” is Easter morning: those who waited saw the great thing — the resurrection — that YHWH did.
17: Thunder and rain in wheat harvest — the dry season (May-June) when rain was virtually unknown in Palestine — is the unseasonable miracle that confirms Samuel’s prophetic authority and the seriousness of Israel’s sin. YHWH’s authority over the agricultural calendar — the very principle of Baal worship, which Israel found attractive, was precisely control of rain and fertility — is demonstrated by the unseasonable rain. Christ’s authority over creation (stilling the storm, Matthew 8:23-27; walking on water, Matthew 14:25) demonstrates the same divine sovereignty over natural forces. The question the disciples ask — “what kind of man is this? even the winds and the waves obey him!” (Matthew 8:27) — is the same astonishment that Samuel’s thunder-and-rain sign produced: “all the people stood in great awe of the LORD and of Samuel” (v18).
18: The people’s response to the supernatural sign — “all the people stood in deep awe of the LORD and of Samuel” (wayyîrəʾû kol-hāʿām məʾōd ʾet-YHWH wəʾet-šəmûʾēl) — is the fear of YHWH that the covenant required. Samuel and YHWH share the people’s fear-response, as Christ and the Father share the disciples’ reverence. After Christ stills the storm, the disciples worship (Matthew 14:33); after the Transfiguration, they fall facedown in fear (Matthew 17:6). The combination of awe for YHWH and for his representative — held together — is the OT framework for the NT’s identification of Jesus with YHWH.
19: The people’s fear-driven request — “pray to the LORD your God for us so we won’t die!” — is the recognition that they need intercessory mediation. They cannot approach YHWH directly in their sin; they need the prophet’s prayer. Samuel as intercessor for sinful Israel is the type of Christ’s eternal intercession: “therefore he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them” (Hebrews 7:25). The people confess (“we have added to all our sins this evil of asking for a king”) and ask for mediated access to God — the exact structure of the gospel: confession + mediator = access to divine grace.
20: Samuel’s reassurance — “do not be afraid. You have committed all this wrong, but do not turn away from the LORD — serve him with your whole heart” — is the gospel word of grace after conviction: you have sinned, but the response is not self-punishment or distance from God but deeper engagement. “Do not be afraid” is the most repeated divine command in the Bible, and it is always spoken after the awareness of unworthiness in the presence of holiness (Isaiah 6:5-7; Luke 5:8-10; Revelation 1:17). The Christ who says “neither do I condemn you; go and sin no more” (John 8:11) is the fulfillment of Samuel’s “do not be afraid — but serve him with your whole heart.”
21: “Do not turn away to pursue empty idols that cannot help or save — they are worthless” (hattōhû lōʾ yôʿîlû wəlōʾ yaṣṣîlû kî-tōhû hēm) — tōhû (emptiness/void) is the word used for pre-creation chaos in Genesis 1:2. Idols are the equivalent of pre-creation void — they offer the appearance of power and help but contain nothing. Isaiah extensively develops this theme (“they are nothing”, Isaiah 44:9; “worthless”, Isaiah 44:10; their makers are “nothing”, Isaiah 44:12). Christ’s exclusive claim — “no one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6) — is the positive assertion of the truth that Samuel’s negative warning anticipates: idols save nothing; YHWH in Christ saves everything.
22: A revelation of God: “The LORD will not abandon his people, because of his great name — it pleased him to make you his own” (kî hôʾîl YHWH laʿăśôt ʾetkem lô ləʿām). YHWH’s faithfulness to his people is grounded not in their merit but in his own name and character — his own decision and delight in them. This is the deepest ground of election and perseverance: God keeps his people because of who he is, not because of what they have done. The NT expression is Romans 11:29: “for God’s gifts and his call are irrevocable,” and John 10:28-29: “no one can snatch them out of my hand... no one can snatch them out of my Father’s hand.” The security of the believer rests on YHWH’s character, which is expressed fully in the faithful love of Christ.
23: A type: Samuel’s intercessory commitment — “far be it from me to sin against the LORD by stopping my prayers for you” (ḥālîlāh lî mēḥăṭōʾ la-YHWH meḥădal lehitpallēl baʿadkem) — defines perpetual intercession as the ongoing work of the prophet. Samuel will continue to pray for Israel even as he hands over his public leadership role. Christ’s perpetual intercession (Hebrews 7:25: “he always lives to intercede”; Romans 8:34: “Christ Jesus... is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us”) is the fulfillment of Samuel’s commitment. The prophet who refuses to stop praying for his people is the type of the High Priest whose intercessory work is eternal and unceasing.
24: The closing charge — “only fear the LORD and serve him faithfully with all your heart — consider the great things he has done for you” — is the distillation of covenant faithfulness: fear, service, wholehearted devotion, and remembrance. These four elements are the core of deuteronomic covenant life (Deuteronomy 6:4-9, 17; 1 Samuel 10:12-13). Christ’s summary of the law (Matthew 22:37-39: love God with heart, soul, mind, and strength; love neighbor as self) is the positive fulfillment of this covenant charge. “Consider the great things he has done for you” — the memory of salvation as the motivation for obedience — is precisely the logic of 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought at a price; therefore honor God with your bodies.”
25: “But if you persist in doing evil, both you and your king will be swept away” — the final covenant warning. The “sweeping away” (tussāpû) of both people and king together is the judgment that the disobedient covenant community brings on itself. The book of Samuel will enact this warning in slow motion: Saul’s disobedience accumulates until he is swept away (1 Samuel 31), and Israel’s persistent unfaithfulness leads eventually to the exile. But the exile is not the end of the covenant story: the one who was swept away in the judgment (“he was cut off from the land of the living”, Isaiah 53:8) rises from that sweeping away to become the foundation of the new covenant community that cannot be swept away (“on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of death will not overcome it”, Matthew 16:18).
